title: US Expands China Tech Blacklist, Europe's Fighter Jet Program Collapses
subhead: Washington adds Alibaba, Baidu, and BYD to its military companies list, while France and Germany abandon their €100 billion sixth-generation fighter project — a double blow to allied tech sovereignty efforts.

sections.heading: US Blacklists Major Chinese Tech Firms as 'Military-Linked'
sections.body:
The Pentagon added Alibaba, Baidu, and BYD to its list of Chinese military companies, expanding the blacklist to 188 firms that Washington believes support Beijing's defense apparatus. The move marks a significant escalation, targeting three of China's most recognizable technology giants — e-commerce leader Alibaba, search engine Baidu, and electric vehicle manufacturer BYD — alongside dozens of other entities.

According to Reuters, the designations restrict US companies and citizens from investing in or providing certain services to these firms, though the practical impact depends on implementation details. Beijing condemned the decision as discriminatory, vowing to defend its interests. The blacklist now spans semiconductors, AI, cloud computing, automotive, and other strategic sectors.

Separately, a bipartisan pair of US senators urged the Trump administration to tighten restrictions on contract chipmakers like TSMC to prevent advanced AI chips from reaching overseas subsidiaries of Chinese companies, Reuters reports. The lawmakers warned that current controls allow Chinese firms to access cutting-edge semiconductors through foreign-based entities, undermining export controls.
sections.whyItMatters: The expansion signals that US-China tech decoupling is accelerating, with Washington now treating China's largest consumer tech firms as extensions of the military-industrial complex. If the TSMC restrictions tighten, Chinese AI development could face severe compute constraints.

sections.heading: China Preps $295 Billion National AI Infrastructure Buildout
sections.body:
China is preparing to spend approximately 2 trillion yuan ($295 billion) over five years to build data centers across the country, challenging US dominance in the AI race, Reuters reports. The plan aims to create nationwide AI infrastructure capable of training and deploying advanced models, with investments spanning compute clusters, energy supply, and networking.

The announcement comes as China's exports surged past forecasts in May, driven by strong demand for chips, electric vehicles, and high-tech goods fueling the global AI boom. Exports rose 19.4% year-on-year, according to Anadolu Agency, defying expectations despite Middle East conflict headwinds. The trade data underscores China's position as a critical link in the global AI supply chain, even as Washington intensifies restrictions.
sections.whyItMatters: China is betting heavily that domestic compute capacity can offset US export controls. If successful, Beijing could reduce its dependence on foreign semiconductors for AI training — but the plan faces questions about energy supply, chip availability, and whether local data centers can match Western efficiency.

sections.heading: France and Germany Cancel €100 Billion FCAS Fighter Jet Program
sections.body:
Germany and France have scrapped the Future Combat Air System (FCAS), Europe's most ambitious collaborative defense project, after years of industrial disputes over technology sharing and intellectual property, Reuters and Defense News report. The €100 billion program, launched in 2017 to develop a sixth-generation fighter with Spain, collapsed due to unresolvable differences between France's Dassault Aviation and Germany's Airbus.

German Defense Minister Boris Pistorius outlined three options: buying additional F-35 jets from the United States, joining the British-Italian-Japanese Global Combat Air Programme (GCAP), or developing a national fighter under German leadership. Simple Flying reports that France will likely pursue an independent sixth-generation fighter rather than join GCAP, as Paris needs a carrier-capable platform.

The cancellation deals a major blow to European defense autonomy. With FCAS gone, Europe now lacks an indigenous path to sixth-generation air power, leaving member states dependent on US platforms or fragmented national programs.
sections.whyItMatters: The FCAS collapse reveals the limits of European defense integration. Industrial rivalry between Airbus and Dassault proved insurmountable, and without a common platform, European air forces will depend on US F-35s or UK-led GCAP — undermining the continent's strategic sovereignty ambitions.

sections.heading: Finland Moves to Allow Nuclear Weapons Transport During Crises
sections.body:
Finland's parliamentary defence committee has backed legislation to allow nuclear weapons transport during crisis situations, YLE reports. The amendment would move Finland's nuclear weapons ban into the criminal code while creating an exception for allied nuclear arms transit — a significant shift for the traditionally non-nuclear Nordic nation.

The government proposal removes a clause in the Nuclear Energy Act that prohibits the import, manufacture, possession, and detonation of nuclear explosives. According to the committee, the change would strengthen Finland's security and the credibility of NATO's nuclear deterrent amid heightened Russian aggression.

The vote was divided along partisan lines: opposition parties including the Social Democrats, Greens, and Left Alliance submitted a dissenting opinion. Once enacted, Finland's nuclear legislation would align with Sweden, Norway, and Denmark.
sections.whyItMatters: The move reflects Finland's rapid integration into NATO's nuclear planning after decades of neutrality. It signals that the alliance's newest members are preparing for the possibility of nuclear escalation in Europe.

sections.heading: China Shares Moon Samples with Russia, Deepening Space Cooperation
sections.body:
China has delivered 1.5 grams of lunar soil collected from the far side of the moon to Russian scientists, the South China Morning Post reports. The samples — the first ever retrieved from the lunar far side via the Chang'e-6 mission — were handed to the Russian Space Research Institute on June 3 as part of a bilateral cooperation agreement.

The institute said researchers will study the composition of lunar material, with particular interest in volatile compounds that may have been preserved in the soil. Identifying these compounds could help understand the moon's history and guide future settlement efforts.

The sample transfer underscores deepening space cooperation between Beijing and Moscow, even as both countries face Western sanctions. China and Russia have previously collaborated on lunar exploration and space science as part of a broader strategic partnership.
sections.whyItMatters: The lunar sample sharing demonstrates that China-Russia scientific cooperation continues despite geopolitical isolation. Access to far-side material — unavailable from US or Soviet missions — gives both countries unique research advantages.

sections.heading: Microsoft Cuts Cloud Jobs in China as Data Laws Tighten
sections.body:
Microsoft is laying off 200-400 employees at its Azure cloud unit in China amid tightening data regulations in both Washington and Beijing, the South China Morning Post reports. Staff in Beijing and Shanghai received termination notices last week, with employment ending July 6. Some were offered relocation to Canada.

This marks at least Microsoft's third round of downsizing in China in two years. The company continues to operate its China cloud business through a local partner, but cross-border data flows have become increasingly restricted as both governments impose stricter controls.

Other Microsoft units including its developer division and AI teams remain unaffected. The company said it remains 'focused on serving customers and growing our business globally.'
sections.whyItMatters: Microsoft's pullback reflects the growing difficulty of operating cloud infrastructure in China. As data sovereignty rules harden, US tech firms face a choice: maintain limited China operations through local partners, or exit entirely.

sections.heading: USA Rare Earth Invests $1.2 Billion in South Carolina Magnet Facility
sections.body:
USA Rare Earth will invest $1.2 billion to build a magnet manufacturing facility in Cherokee County, South Carolina, creating approximately 490 jobs, Business Facilities reports. The facility will produce sintered neodymium-iron-boron (NdFeB) permanent magnets and refined rare earth metals, targeting 6,400 metric tons per year of magnet production capacity.

The South Carolina site complements USA Rare Earth's existing operations in Oklahoma, Texas, Colorado, and planned facilities in Brazil, the UK, and France. Engineering work is underway with commissioning targeted for 2028. The company cited proximity to defense and aerospace customers, reliable power, and workforce availability as key factors.

The investment is part of a broader US effort to rebuild domestic rare earth processing capacity. China currently dominates global production of these critical minerals, which are essential for electric vehicles, wind turbines, and defense systems.
sections.whyItMatters: The facility advances US efforts to reduce dependence on Chinese rare earths. But with commissioning targeted for 2028 and China controlling 80%+ of global processing, the strategic gap will persist for years.

bottomLine: Today's developments reveal competing trajectories in the tech-power competition. The US is tightening its chokehold on Chinese tech, expanding blacklists and pressuring Taiwan to restrict AI chip exports. China is responding with massive domestic infrastructure investment and deeper cooperation with Russia — from lunar samples to technology coordination. Meanwhile, Europe's fighter jet program collapsed, exposing the limits of allied defense integration. Finland's nuclear weapons law change and the Quad's focus on critical minerals both reflect hardening positions. The pattern is clear: technology sovereignty is being pursued through unilateral action, not cooperation.
sources: Reuters, Defense News, Simple Flying, South China Morning Post, YLE, Anadolu Agency, Business Facilities, UPI, AP News, Bloomberg
